data.title: Is Deutsche Lufthansa (XTRA:LHA) Offering Value After Recent Share Price Pullback?
data.description:
To make sense of whether Deutsche Lufthansa is attractively priced today, you first need a clear picture of what the recent share moves and fundamentals are actually telling you about value and risk.

Over the last week the stock has seen a 4.3% decline, while the 30 day return is a 1.4% decline, set against a 12.8% decline year to date and a 22.0% gain over the past year. This mix can leave you wondering whether the market is reassessing the company or simply cooling after a stronger run.

Recent news coverage has focused on Deutsche Lufthansa's ongoing role as a major European carrier and its exposure to global travel trends, as well as operational developments that influence investor sentiment on costs and capacity. Together, these factors help frame why the share price has been moving the way it has and why opinions on its risk and reward profile may be shifting.

Simply Wall St currently gives Deutsche Lufthansa a valuation score of 5 out of 6, which suggests most of its fundamental checks flag the stock as undervalued. The rest of this article will walk through traditional valuation methods before coming back to a more complete way of thinking about what that score really means for you.

Find out why Deutsche Lufthansa's 22.0% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Deutsche Lufthansa Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes forecasts of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, to estimate what the entire business might be worth right now.

For Deutsche Lufthansa, the Simply Wall St model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach built on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of €318.6m, so the story here is about what future cash generation could look like rather than current cash strength.

Analyst and extrapolated estimates point to free cash flow of €1.69b in 2026 and around €1.86b by 2030, with intermediate years such as 2027, 2028 and 2029 also projected in the €1.43b to €2.11b range. These are all expressed in today’s money through discounting, then summed to get an equity value.

On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of €20.40 per share. Compared with the current share price, this implies the stock is 63.3% undervalued according to this framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Deutsche Lufthansa is undervalued by 63.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 237 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Deutsche Lufthansa.

Approach 2: Deutsche Lufthansa Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much you are paying for each €1 of earnings. This makes it a practical tool when you want to compare opportunities quickly. What counts as a “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risks, with higher growth and lower perceived risk typically justifying a higher multiple.

Deutsche Lufthansa currently trades on a P/E of 6.74x. This sits below the Airlines industry average P/E of 8.34x and well below the broader peer group average of 13.50x, so the market is currently assigning it a lower earnings multiple than many comparables.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Deutsche Lufthansa is 15.34x. This is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be based on factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks. Because it blends these inputs, the Fair Ratio can give a more tailored benchmark than simply lining the stock up against industry or peer averages.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 15.34x with the actual P/E of 6.74x suggests the shares trade below this model-based estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Safran (ENXTPA:SAF) After Its Strong Multi Year Run?
data.description:
• Wondering if Safran at €272.50 is still offering value after a strong multi year run, or if the easy gains are behind it? This article focuses squarely on what the current price could mean for you.
  • The stock is currently around €272.50, after a 25.6% return over 1 year and a 101.1% return over 3 years. Recent returns of 11.8% over 7 days, 5.2% over 30 days and 11.3% year to date show a pullback that may reflect shifting views on risk and reward.
  • Recent coverage of Safran has focused on its position in the aerospace and defense sector, including ongoing attention on order activity, program execution and wider industry sentiment, which often influences how investors think about long term cash flows. At the same time, commentary around the sector's capital intensity and sensitivity to contract cycles has kept valuation firmly in focus for many shareholders.
  • Safran currently has a valuation score of 6 out of 6. The rest of this article walks through what that means across multiple valuation methods before finishing with a way to see beyond the numbers to the broader valuation story.

Safran delivered 25.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Aerospace & Defense industry.

Approach 1: Safran Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of the cash a business could generate in the future, then discounts those amounts back to what they might be worth today.

For Safran, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about €3.96b. Analyst inputs extend through 2030, with projected free cash flow of €7.50b by then, and further estimates are extrapolated beyond the explicit analyst horizon by Simply Wall St. For the 2026 to 2035 period, the model incorporates a series of annual free cash flow projections, each discounted back to today in € terms.

On this basis, the DCF output suggests an estimated intrinsic value of about €394.19 per share. Against the current share price of roughly €272.50, this implies a discount of 30.9%, which points to the shares trading below the model’s estimate of underlying value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Safran Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link what you pay per share to the earnings the business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many euros investors are paying today for each euro of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on factors like how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how uncertain those earnings are. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with a lower multiple.

Safran currently trades on a P/E of 15.76x. This is below both the Aerospace & Defense industry average of 51.53x and a peer group average of 31.44x.   • Q1 2026 net interest margin sits at 4.16% and the cost to income ratio is 58.61%, while trailing net profit margin over the last year is 26.1% compared with 19.8% in the prior year.
  • Analysts' consensus view links improving profitability to factors such as cost control and digital banking, and these margin figures give mixed support to that, with:
    • a relatively stable NIM around 4.1% across recent quarters, which matches the idea of disciplined pricing and funding costs, and
    • a cost to income ratio near 59%, which shows some efficiency but also aligns with commentary about higher compensation and software expenses that can keep pressure on operating leverage.

Loan book near US$6.8b with credit costs in focus

  • Total loans stand at about US$6.8b in Q1 2026, and non performing loans are US$39.6 million compared with a recent range around US$38.8 million to US$43.0 million.
  • Bears argue that ongoing issues in areas such as small ticket leasing could keep credit costs elevated, and the current data partly speaks to that, because:
    • non performing loans remain in the tens of millions, which fits concerns about pockets of weaker credit quality, yet
    • the latest figure is not at the top of the recent range, which challenges the idea that credit stress is rapidly worsening right now based on these reported numbers.
On credit quality and loan growth, skeptics point to leasing charge offs and local concentration risk as key watchpoints, and this earnings set gives a numerical starting point for weighing those concerns against actual non performing loan levels and a loan book now close to US$6.8b. 🐻 Peoples Bancorp Bear Case

Shareholders in SG Company S.p.A. (BIT:SGC) may be thrilled to learn that the covering analyst has just delivered a major upgrade to their near-term forecasts. Consensus estimates suggest investors could expect greatly increased statutory revenues and earnings per share, with the analyst modelling a real improvement in business performance.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Following the upgrade, the most recent consensus for SG from its lone analyst is for revenues of €56m in 2026 which, if met, would be a notable 12% increase on its sales over the past 12 months. The losses are expected to disappear over the next year or so, with forecasts for a profit of €0.076 per share this year. Before this latest update, the analyst had been forecasting revenues of €50m and earnings per share (EPS) of €0.046 in 2026. So we can see there's been a pretty clear increase in analyst sentiment in recent times, with both revenues and earnings per share receiving a decent lift in the latest estimates.

View our latest analysis for SG

It will come as no surprise to learn that the analyst has increased their price target for SG 6.7% to €0.48 on the back of these upgrades.

Looking at the bigger picture now, one of the ways we can make sense of these forecasts is to see how they measure up against both past performance and industry growth estimates. It's pretty clear that there is an expectation that SG's revenue growth will slow down substantially, with revenues to the end of 2026 expected to display 12% growth on an annualised basis. This is compared to a historical growth rate of 33% over the past five years. Compare this to the 25 other companies in this industry with analyst coverage, which are forecast to grow their revenue at 10% per year. Factoring in the forecast slowdown in growth, it looks like SG is forecast to grow at about the same rate as the wider industry.

The Bottom Line

The most important thing to take away from this upgrade is that the analyst upgraded their earnings per share estimates for this year, expecting improving business conditions. They also upgraded their revenue forecasts, although the latest estimates suggest that SG will grow in line with the overall market. With a serious upgrade to expectations and a rising price target, it might be time to take another look at SG.

The covering analyst is definitely bullish on SG, but no company is perfect. Indeed, you should know that there are several potential concerns to be aware of, including dilutive stock issuance over the past year. For anyone who wants to understand Asker Healthcare Group's profit beyond the statutory numbers, it's important to note that during the last twelve months statutory profit was reduced by kr171m due to unusual items. While deductions due to unusual items are disappointing in the first instance, there is a silver lining. data.title: Don't Race Out To Buy Singapore Technologies Engineering Ltd (SGX:S63) Just Because It's Going Ex-Dividend
data.description:
Singapore Technologies Engineering Ltd (SGX:S63) stock is about to trade ex-dividend in 4 days. The ex-dividend date is commonly two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Thus, you can purchase Singapore Technologies Engineering's shares before the 28th of April in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 13th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be S$0.11 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed S$0.18 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Singapore Technologies Engineering has a trailing yield of 1.6% on the current share price of S$11.25. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Singapore Technologies Engineering's dividend is reliable and sustainable. So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Singapore Technologies Engineering distributed an unsustainably high 121% of its profit as dividends to shareholders last year. Without more sustainable payment behaviour, the dividend looks precarious. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Singapore Technologies Engineering generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. It distributed 47% of its free cash flow as dividends, a comfortable payout level for most companies.

It's disappointing to see that the dividend was not covered by profits, but cash is more important from a dividend sustainability perspective, and Singapore Technologies Engineering fortunately did generate enough cash to fund its dividend. Still, if the company repeatedly paid a dividend greater than its profits, we'd be concerned. Extraordinarily few companies are capable of persistently paying a dividend that is greater than their profits.

Check out our latest analysis for Singapore Technologies Engineering

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

When earnings decline, dividend companies become much harder to analyse and own safely. If earnings fall far enough, the company could be forced to cut its dividend. That's why it's not ideal to see Singapore Technologies Engineering's earnings per share have been shrinking at 2.5% a year over the previous five years.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. In the last 10 years, Singapore Technologies Engineering has lifted its dividend by approximately 1.8% a year on average.

To Sum It Up

Should investors buy Singapore Technologies Engineering for the upcoming dividend? It's not a great combination to see a company with earnings in decline and paying out 121% of its profits, which could imply the dividend may be at risk of being cut in the future. However, the cash payout ratio was much lower - good news from a dividend perspective - which makes us wonder why there is such a mis-match between income and cashflow. It's not the most attractive proposition from a dividend perspective, and we'd probably give this one a miss for now.

So if you're still interested in Singapore Technologies Engineering despite it's poor dividend qualities, you should be well informed on some of the risks facing this stock. For example, we've found 3 warning signs for Singapore Technologies Engineering that we recommend you consider before investing in the business.

Generally, we wouldn't recommend just buying the first dividend stock you see. Here's a curated list of interesting stocks that are strong dividend payers.
